Name: latency_optimized_jacobian_layer_skipping
Title: Optimizing Latency in Neural Inference via Jacobian-Based Layer Skipping
Short Hypothesis: This proposal hypothesizes that a Jacobian-based layer-skipping mechanism can dynamically optimize latency during neural network inference by selectively activating layers based on local activation gradients, thus minimizing unnecessary computations while maintaining accuracy.
Related Work: While existing works such as LAUDNet and HPIPE integrate layer skipping and dynamic computation, they often rely on static profiling or heuristic methods that may not fully exploit real-time activation dynamics. This proposal distinguishes itself by leveraging Jacobian matrices to inform layer selection, ensuring a theoretically grounded approach that adapts to input variability, thus providing a more dynamic and efficient inference mechanism.
Abstract: In the pursuit of efficient neural network inference, this proposal introduces a novel Jacobian-based layer-skipping framework designed to minimize latency while preserving computational integrity. Traditional dynamic inference techniques often rely on static profiling or heuristic approaches, which can lead to suboptimal resource utilization. Our proposed method leverages the local gradients of activations, computed through the Jacobian matrix, to determine which layers are critical for the current input. By implementing a real-time decision-making process for layer activation based on these gradients, we aim to achieve a latency-aware inference system that dynamically adapts to different input conditions. Our approach will be empirically validated through a series of benchmarks across various neural architectures, measuring throughput and accuracy while explicitly accounting for latency and memory bandwidth usage. This work not only advances the theoretical understanding of test-time optimization but also positions itself within the framework of PAC-Bayes stability bounds, illustrating a novel intersection of machine learning theory and practical inference challenges.
Experiments: Implement the Jacobian-based layer-skipping mechanism in a standard neural network architecture (e.g., ResNet or Transformer).
Conduct experiments across a diverse set of datasets to assess the model's performance under varying input conditions.
Evaluate latency and accuracy trade-offs by measuring wall-clock times and accuracy metrics before and after implementing the layer-skipping mechanism.
Compare the proposed method against existing dynamic inference methods, such as LAUDNet, using standardized benchmarks.
Analyze the relationship between layer activation decisions and input characteristics by visualizing Jacobian contributions.
Risk Factors and Limitations: Potential risks include the challenge of accurately computing Jacobians in real-time without introducing significant overhead, and the reliance on the assumption that local gradients will sufficiently guide optimal layer selection. Additionally, the method's performance may vary across different neural architectures and datasets, necessitating extensive testing for generalization.
